name: kohlrabi

supplementary_research.finding: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products (isothiocyanates) from Brassica vegetables including kohlrabi activate Nrf2 antioxidant pathways and have demonstrated chemopreventive activity in multiple cancer models; PMID 28202866.

Epidemiological meta-analysis (2020) found cruciferous vegetable intake inversely associated with all-cause mortality (HR 0.90 per serving/day) and cardiovascular mortality, supporting kohlrabi's role in a longevity-oriented diet; PMID 31960481.
Kohlrabi provides ~62 mg vitamin C per 100 g (raw), contributing to collagen synthesis, immune defence, and attenuation of oxidative stress — all mechanisms linked to healthy ageing.

seasonality: Spring and autumn; available year-round in temperate climates

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Water-soluble; best preserved raw or lightly steamed. Provides ~69% of RDA per 100 g.
Hydrolysed by myrosinase (activated by chewing or chopping) to bioactive isothiocyanates. Heat destroys myrosinase — eat raw or add minced raw kohlrabi after cooking to preserve activity.
Supports blood-pressure regulation and cardiovascular health. Bioavailability is high across preparation methods.
Inulin-type fructans support gut microbiome diversity and short-chain fatty acid production, linked to reduced inflammation and metabolic health.

synergies.description: Mustard seed provides exogenous myrosinase. When combined with cooked kohlrabi (which has heat-inactivated myrosinase), it restores glucosinolate conversion to isothiocyanates, maximising chemopreventive potential.
Fat-soluble carotenoids in kohlrabi leaves (lutein, beta-carotene) are significantly better absorbed with dietary fat; olive oil is the traditional pairing.

Kohlrabi is a traditional base for kimchi and Eastern European ferments; fermentation increases bioavailability of glucosinolate breakdown products and adds probiotic benefit aligned with gut-longevity research.
